metadata.title: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself

beats.narration_text: Send electrons through a double slit one at a time — never two in the machine at once. Each one lands as a single dot. After thousands of dots, an interference pattern assembles itself. One particle interfered with itself.

props.segment: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
props.command: can one electron interfere with itself?

props.segment: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: classical water waves through two slits — two wavefronts overlap, bright bands where they reinforce, dark bands where they cancel"

beats.narration_text: Water waves through two slits: classic interference. The two wavefronts overlap and add up — bright where they reinforce, dark where they cancel.
Two waves need two things in the machine at once. If there's only one electron at a time — nothing to interfere with — classical physics predicts a smooth, featureless smear. Not fringes.

card.copy: One electron at a time: no partner to interfere with. Classical predicts a smear. The experiment shows fringes. Why?

props.segment: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: single electron landing as a dot — one click, one localized spot on the detector"

beats.narration_text: Each electron lands as a single dot — a click, a spot, one definite place. That part is perfectly particle-like.

props.segment: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: single electron wavefunction passing through both slits simultaneously — two wavefronts spreading from each slit and overlapping"

beats.narration_text: But the wave function of that single electron passed through both slits. It spread out from each slit and interfered with itself on the far side.

props.segment: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: interference of the electron wavefunction with itself — probability density shows bright and dark fringes, dot most likely to land on bright fringe"

beats.narration_text: Where the two wavefronts reinforce, the probability density is high. Where they cancel, the probability is nearly zero. The dot is most likely to land on a bright fringe.

props.segment: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: double-slit pattern building up dot by dot — show accumulation after 10, 100, 1000 electrons, fringes emerging from individually random dots"

beats.narration_text: As electrons accumulate — ten, then thousands — the dot pattern fills in. The histogram of where they land matches the wave interference pattern exactly.

props.segment: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: de Broglie wavelength labeling the fringe spacing — lambda = h/p for 54 eV electrons gives 0.167 nm, fringe spacing set by wavelength and slit separation"

beats.narration_text: This is the de Broglie wavelength in action: lambda equals h over p. The electron's momentum determines the fringe spacing, just as a wavelength would.

props.segment: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: 54 eV electron double-slit worked example — calculate de Broglie wavelength 0.167 nm, show this is comparable to atomic spacing, relate to fringe pattern"

beats.narration_text: Illustrative example: electrons at 54 eV have a de Broglie wavelength of about 0.17 nanometers — comparable to atomic spacing. That wavelength sets the fringe spacing you'd measure.
The electron lands in one spot. Its wave function traveled both paths. The probability of each spot comes from the wave — and the wave is the whole electron, all of it, all at once.

card.copy: One electron. Both slits. One dot. The wave was the whole electron.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. One electron at a time, simulated. Ask Claude to build the dot-by-dot pattern. Watch the wave emerge from individual clicks.

props.segment: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
props.command: simulate the double-slit experiment building up dot by dot — for 54 eV electrons and slit separation 1 micrometer, plot the accumulating pattern after 10, 100, 1000, and 5000 electrons

beats.narration_text: If this doubled your questions, subscribe. Medhavy.

props.title: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself.
props.subline: subscribe · the wave was the whole electron
